title: Mirror spaces from formal deformation of Lagrangians and their gluing.
speaker: Hansol Hong
abstract: For given a Lagrangian in a symplectic manifold, one can consider deformation of A-infinity algebra structures on its Floer complex by degree 1 elements satisfying the Maurer-Cartan equation. The space of such degree 1 elements can be thought of as giving a local chart of the mirror. In this talk, I will explain how to glue local charts from different Lagrangians using isomorphisms between Lagrangians in the Fukaya category. As an application, we will discuss the mirror construction for Gr(2,4) that recovers its Lie-theoretical mirror.

proof_stages.label: Mirror as complexified Lagrangian deformation
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces mirror symmetry and extracts from the SYZ picture the principle that a mirror chart should be the complexified deformation space of a fixed Lagrangian.

proof_stages.label: Maurer-Cartan formal deformation chart
proof_stages.summary: Formal deformations are defined by inserting degree-one elements b into the A-infinity operations of the Lagrangian Floer complex, and the Maurer-Cartan equation cuts out the local chart MC_L.

proof_stages.label: Local categorical mirror from the universal deformation
proof_stages.summary: The deformed Floer differential against the universal Maurer-Cartan element gives a superpotential and an A-infinity functor from the Fukaya category to matrix factorizations, or in Z-graded settings to coherent sheaf-type categories over MC_L.

proof_stages.label: Noncommutative multi-Lagrangian chart and the gluing problem
proof_stages.summary: A union of two Lagrangian spheres in the deformed conifold yields a quiver path algebra whose Maurer-Cartan relations form the noncommutative crepant resolution, motivating the replacement by several single-Lagrangian commutative charts that must be glued.

proof_stages.label: Categorical gluing criterion for MC charts
proof_stages.summary: Two points in two Maurer-Cartan charts are identified when the corresponding deformed Fukaya objects are isomorphic, and such identifications produce a glued B-side category via a homotopy fiber product of local categories.

proof_stages.label: Wall-crossing as an isomorphism computation
proof_stages.summary: In a two-dimensional conic-fibration model, modified torus fibers are made to intersect and the condition that a degree-zero morphism alpha be closed and invertible gives the standard wall-crossing coordinate change.

proof_stages.label: Completion by the immersed singular-fiber chart
proof_stages.summary: Gluing the two C-star-squared torus charts leaves a missing point, which is filled by adding the Maurer-Cartan chart of the immersed S2 singular fiber with coordinates u and v.

proof_stages.label: Global conifold and Grassmannian mirror constructions
proof_stages.summary: The local gluing calculation is applied to the deformed conifold to obtain the resolved conifold mirror and, via a Plucker/S1-reduction to a conifold model, to Gr(2,4) to recover the missing critical points of the full Landau-Ginzburg mirror.

examples.name: SYZ Lagrangian torus fibration
examples.note: The SYZ dual-torus construction is used to extract the guiding idea that a mirror point is a Lagrangian together with a local system, hence a complexified Lagrangian deformation.
examples.engagement_reason: The SYZ torus fibration is used as the standard motivating construction and described as dualizing torus fibers with local systems, but no concrete instance is computed or checked.

passes.reason: A general SYZ Lagrangian torus fibration is used as the standard mirror-construction paradigm, not as a structurally special failure case.
A SYZ Lagrangian torus fibration is the standard constructive mirror-symmetry setup, with no structural extremality or adversarial use here.

examples.name: Pair of pants with immersed circle
examples.note: The three-punctured sphere with an immersed circle having three self-intersections makes the Maurer-Cartan equation, superpotential W=xyz, and matrix-factorization functor concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: For the pair of pants he draws the immersed circle, names the three self-intersections, forms the deformation parameter, checks the Maurer-Cartan equation via cancellation of M2 terms, computes the M3 contribution as xyz, and relates it to a matrix factorization.

passes.reason: The pair of pants is a minimal surface building block and the chosen immersed circle has reflection symmetry used in the Maurer-Cartan computation.
The pair of pants with the three-self-intersection immersed circle is used constructively, and its reflection symmetry is explicitly what makes the Maurer-Cartan computation simplify.
rigid minimal three-punctured sphere with a reflection-symmetric Seidel immersed circle, used as a toy computation
The pair of pants with the chosen immersed circle is a highly regular threefold-symmetric setup, explicitly using reflection symmetry in the disk count.
the chosen immersed circle on the three-punctured sphere has reflection/cyclic symmetry used in the disk-count cancellation, but serves as an illustrative computation
The chosen immersed circle is structurally notable for the reflection symmetry used in the Floer calculation.

The pair of pants with the three-crossing immersed circle is a toy model for the deformation construction, and the chosen immersed circle is structurally regular with reflection symmetry causing cancellations.
Used as a simple positive computation; the three-punctured sphere is a minimal pair-of-pants building block and the chosen immersed circle has reflection symmetry.
Used as a concrete Floer-theoretic mirror chart, and the three-punctured pair-of-pants configuration with the immersed circle is explicitly distinguished by reflection/regular symmetry.
Used as a simple illustrative LG-mirror computation; the thrice-punctured sphere is a minimal no-moduli surface and the chosen immersed circle has the reflection symmetry used for cancellations.
the reflection-symmetric immersed circle in the pair of pants is used as a toy computation of the Maurer-Cartan potential, not as a refutation.
minimal pair-of-pants surface with a reflection-symmetric three-crossing immersed circle used to compute the mirror potential.

examples.name: Deformed conifold and its resolution
examples.note: The conifold calculation shows that using several Lagrangian components gives a quiver/noncommutative crepant resolution, thereby motivating the need to glue commutative single-Lagrangian charts.
examples.engagement_reason: For the deformed conifold he writes explicit equations, analyzes the double conic fibration, constructs Lagrangian spheres and their quiver variables, computes Maurer-Cartan relations such as xyz-cyx, and explains how gluing yields the commutative resolved conifold.

passes.reason: The deformed/resolved conifold is a highly regular quadric/toric local Calabi-Yau model used to demonstrate mirror gluing.
The conifold smoothing/small resolution is a boundary local Calabi-Yau transition model around the ordinary node, used to build the mirror rather than refute a claim.
conifold transition is a nodal limiting local Calabi-Yau with a small crepant resolution, used as a positive gluing model
The deformed/resolved conifold is a very regular local Calabi–Yau model, but it is used constructively to illustrate gluing rather than to refute a claim.
the deformed/resolved conifold is the highly regular determinant/ordinary-double-point local model used as a gluing test case, not to refute a claim
The conifold setup is a smoothing and small resolution of the simplest nodal degeneration.

The deformed/resolved conifold is used as a standard positive test case for noncommutative and commutative mirror gluing, not as a refutation; it is the highly symmetric local conifold model rather than a sporadic exception.
Used as a positive model for gluing mirrors; the conifold transition is the small crepant boundary local model of an ordinary double point and has large quadric/toric symmetry.
Used as a standard conifold-transition example for gluing mirror charts, not to refute a claim, and it belongs to a systematic local Calabi–Yau model rather than a sporadic or extremal case.
Used constructively as the standard local conifold-transition model whose gluing gives the resolved conifold; it is special but not a sporadic exception or counterexample here.
the deformed/resolved conifold is a highly regular local Calabi-Yau at a nodal crepant-transition boundary, used constructively as a mirror example.
conifold smoothing and crepant resolution form a highly symmetric boundary local model for a conifold transition used constructively.

examples.name: Grassmannian Gr(2,4) mirror
examples.note: The Grassmannian example applies the local conifold-style gluing and immersed-fiber chart to recover missing critical points in the full Landau-Ginzburg mirror.
examples.engagement_reason: For Gr(2,4) he uses the Gelfand-Cetlin setup, writes the Plücker relation, performs a reduction to the deformed conifold picture, chooses an immersed Lagrangian with deformation variables, and explains how this recovers missing critical points of the mirror.

passes.reason: Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous symmetric Grassmannian and is used to show the torus-fiber mirror construction misses critical points without extra gluing charts.
Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous Hermitian-symmetric Grassmannian, here used as a positive test case for recovering the full Landau-Ginzburg mirror.
homogeneous Grassmannian mirror is used to exhibit critical points missed by the earlier torus-degeneration chart
Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous Grassmannian, and its mirror is used to expose the missing critical points in the toric-degeneration/partial mirror construction.
Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous Grassmannian/Klein-quadric case, and here it exposes that the toric-degeneration generic-fiber mirror misses critical points
Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous Grassmannian used as an application of the gluing construction.
The Gr(2,4) mirror is used as a case study where gluing recovers missing critical points, and Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous Grassmannian with large symmetry but not an exceptional classification outlier.
Used as a final application recovering missing critical points; Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous Grassmannian symmetric space, not a sporadic exception.
Used as an application recovering the full Landau–Ginzburg mirror, and Gr(2,4) is a highly homogeneous Grassmannian but not sporadic, degenerate, or extremal.
Used as an application where gluing recovers the full Grassmannian mirror and missing critical points; Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous highly symmetric space.
the homogeneous Grassmannian mirror is used as the case where the torus-fiber/toric-degeneration mirror misses critical points, motivating the gluing construction.
Gr(2,4) is a homogeneous Hermitian symmetric Grassmannian whose mirror is used as an application to recover missing critical points.
